ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (chicken meat, cooked) used as closest defensible parent anchor; no specific stir-fried ginger chicken code exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant or hawker serving of ginger chicken is approximately 300 g (1 plate), providing around 612 kcal, 43.8 g protein, and 43.2 g fat per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Ginger Chicken — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Ginger Chicken (cr_id F11020034). Secondary micronutrients (sugar, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chicken stir-fry with ginger as supporting evidence where HPB data was not available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed chicken dish with low-carbohydrate sauce profile
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ginger chicken stir-fry in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous mixed chicken dish with soy-based sauce and minimal carbohydrate content (3.7 g/100g). Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: (35 × 11.1 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 3.9, rounded to 4.6 accounting for sauce carbohydrates. This is consistent with low-GI protein-dominant dishes documented in Atkinson et al. (2021), Am J Clin Nutr.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 659 mg/100g — exceeds the 600 mg/100g high-concern threshold for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request preparation with reduced soy sauce and no oyster sauce, or substitute low-sodium soy sauce, to bring sodium intake below 400 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (3.7 g/100g) and high protein (14.6 g/100g) support glycaemic control, but high sodium (659 mg/100g) is a secondary concern given the elevated cardiovascular risk in type 2 diabetes
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ginger chicken is suitable for blood glucose management due to its low carbohydrate and low GI profile; however, individuals with type 2 diabetes should monitor sodium intake carefully and avoid pairing with large portions of white rice to prevent overall glycaemic load elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (659 mg/100g) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low at 2.0 g/100g and trans fat is negligible at 0.03 g/100g, which are favourable
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion to 100–150 g and request low-sodium preparation; the low saturated fat and negligible trans fat profile are favourable, but the high sodium content requires active management to avoid blood pressure elevation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ginger (gingerols) provides documented anti-inflammatory benefit; high sodium (659 mg/100g) may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ginger chicken may offer anti-inflammatory benefit from gingerols in ginger; however, individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce to lower sodium and minimise sodium-driven tissue inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ginger Chicken (姜葱鸡, Ayam Halia) is a popular Singaporean Chinese stir-fried dish consisting of bite-sized chicken pieces marinated and cooked with fresh ginger, spring onion, garlic, soy sauce, and oyster sauce, typically finished with a drizzle of sesame oil. The dish is characterised by a savoury, slightly pungent flavour profile with the warmth of ginger and the umami depth of soy and oyster sauces. The texture is tender and moist from the stir-fry technique, with a lightly caramelised exterior from high-heat wok cooking. Ginger chicken is a staple at Singaporean hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and Chinese restaurants, commonly served over steamed white rice or alongside other dishes in a shared meal. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 204 kcal, 14.6 g protein, 14.4 g fat (of which only 2.0 g is saturated), and 3.7 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with a low glycaemic index estimated at 35. Sodium is elevated at 659 mg/100g due to soy sauce and oyster sauce, which is the primary clinical concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The ginger content contributes gingerols, which have well-documented anti-inflammatory properties relevant to conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is halal-certified in most hawker and restaurant preparations and is suitable for ketogenic and high-protein dietary patterns. Calcium (66 mg/100g) and iron (1.1 mg/100g) contribute meaningfully to micronutrient intake.
common_user_queries: How many calories in ginger chicken?
Is ginger chicken suitable for diabetics?
Is ginger chicken high in sodium?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11020034, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Secondary micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, which reduces overall confidence for the GI component only; all other nutritional data is high-confidence from the primary approved source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this specific ginger chicken preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as reported by HPB (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database for this preparation). trans_fat_g is 0.03 g/100g as reported by the input source; this is a trace level consistent with minor trans fat from cooking oils. GI value of 35 is an analogous estimate based on mixed chicken dish with soy-based sauce profile from the University of Sydney GI Database framework; no direct measurement exists for ginger chicken stir-fry. Sugar (1.67 g/100g), cholesterol (40 mg/100g), saturated fat (2.0 g/100g), trans fat (0.03 g/100g), and vitamin C (1.67 mg/100g) were originally sourced from GrabFood in the input record (confidence 0.7); these values have been retained as plausible and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for chicken stir-fry dishes, where they fall within expected ranges. Halal status reflects common hawker and restaurant preparation standards in Singapore.
